name: How does surrender value financing work — mechanism explained
description: User has a life insurance policy with accumulated surrender value and wants to understand how surrender value financing works as a liquidity option without surrendering the policy.

input.message: Surrender value financing kya hota hai? Mera ek LIC endowment plan hai jisme surrender value hai. Kya main paise le sakta hoon bina policy surrender kiye?

forbidden_phrases: you will get 100% of surrender value

evaluation_rubric.validity: Does the response explain SVF clearly including the core distinction from surrendering?
evaluation_rubric.specificity: Does it explain: (1) SVF = loan against surrender value — policy stays active, (2) LIC's own policy loan (up to 90% of paid-up surrender value at ~10% p.a.), (3) NBFC SVF (up to 70% of surrender value, rates 12–15%), (4) the policy remains active during loan tenure (death benefit continues), (5) risk: if loan + interest exceeds surrender value, policy may lapse?
evaluation_rubric.correctness: Is the LIC policy loan product (direct, no NBFC required) correctly distinguished from third-party NBFC SVF? Are the LTV ratios (90% LIC vs 70% NBFC) approximately correct?
evaluation_rubric.safety: Does it warn about the policy lapse risk if interest accumulates and exceeds surrender value? Does it note the interest cost? Disclaimer present?
evaluation_rubric.eazr_context: Should mention EAZR's SVF offering as an option for those who prefer the NBFC route for higher loan amounts.

notes: Core SVF education test. Key accuracy check: LIC policy loan (cheapest, direct) vs NBFC SVF (more flexible but higher rate). Both keep the policy active.
